topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The sterile white walls of the clinic felt like a cage. Elias sat on the edge of the leather couch, his hands clenched so tightly his knuckles were white islands against his skin. Dr. Vance, once the architect of his most painful school years, now regarded him with a calm, almost unnerving empathy. "Tell me again about the incident with the locker," Vance prompted, his voice a low, even murmur that seemed to vibrate in the quiet room. Elias swallowed, the dryness in his throat making the action a conscious effort. He stared at the polished floor, watching a dust mote dance in a shaft of afternoon light. Every carefully constructed response felt like another brick being laid in a structure he couldn't escape. He wanted to bolt, to scream, but the silence in the room held him fast, a heavy, suffocating blanket woven from old memories and present discomfort.

[story 2]

I kept glancing at the door, anticipating the familiar shadow. When Mark walked in, the air in the waiting room seemed to thicken, charged with a tension I couldn't shake. He looked different now—softer lines around his eyes, a tailored suit replacing the perpetually scuffed jacket he used to wear. He offered a practiced, gentle smile. "Ready to talk, Alex?" he asked, his tone perfectly modulated, devoid of the sharp mockery I remembered so vividly. I nodded once, my gaze dropping immediately to my shoes. My breath hitched, shallow and quick. The familiar knot tightened in my chest, a cold, hard pressure behind my ribs. I felt utterly pinned down by the weight of his presence, unable to move, unable to formulate a sentence that didn't sound like a plea.

[story 3]

The scent of lavender and old paper clung to the consultation room, a cloying perfume that made my skin crawl. I fiddled with the cuff of my sweater, the fabric rough against my fingertips. Dr. Vance leaned forward, resting his elbows on his knees, his expression open and inviting. "It takes courage to revisit those foundational hurts," he observed, his voice warm, almost paternal. I felt a strange resistance rising within me, a desperate urge to shatter the delicate composure he had so carefully cultivated. My jaw ached from clenching it so hard. Every word felt monumental, weighted with years of unspoken resentment and lingering fear. I just wanted the encounter to end, to dissolve back into the anonymity of the outside world.
